instruction: Classify the farmer's described crop problem into a cause category.
baseline_prompt: You are a crop-advisory assistant. Classify the farmer's described crop problem into a cause category. Classify into one of these categories: pest, disease, nutrient, water_stress, weather_damage, weed.
source: Authored for TEI-Bench (Sonnet-generated, Sonnet-validated; kept only label-agreed examples).

train.query: Something is boring into the stems of my squash plants. I can see entry holes with what looks like sawdust coming out, and the vines are starting to wilt even though I'm watering regularly.

I'm seeing white powdery coating all over the leaves of my cucumber vines. The leaves look like they've been dusted with flour, and some of the younger leaves are becoming twisted and stunted in growth.

Orange trees in my grove have new leaves coming in very light colored, almost white between the veins. Fruit production has dropped significantly over the past two seasons and the existing fruits are smaller than normal.

The corn leaves are rolling inward and have a grayish tint to them. We haven't had rain in five weeks and the irrigation system broke down two weeks ago. The lower leaves are starting to dry out completely.

We had marble-sized ice fall from the sky for about fifteen minutes this afternoon. My apple orchard took a beating - fruit has dents and bruises all over, leaves are shredded with holes punched through them, and small branches are broken everywhere.
Bermuda grass is invading my vegetable garden beds from the edges and spreading inward. It's creating a thick mat that's suffocating my pepper and eggplant seedlings.
My stored grain has become infested with small brown beetles. They're everywhere in the bags and I can see larvae and damaged kernels. The infestation seems to be spreading to other storage areas.

The grape vines are showing irregular yellow-brown patches on leaves that eventually dry out and fall off, leaving holes. I also notice grayish fuzzy growth on some of the fruit clusters, especially after the recent humid weather.
The rice paddy shows poor tillering and the leaves have a general pale appearance. We rotated from sugarcane last season and didn't do a soil test before planting. Plants seem weak overall.
My tomato plants are wilting badly even though the soil feels wet all the time. The leaves are turning yellow from the bottom up and some roots look dark and mushy when I checked them yesterday.

An unexpected cold snap hit us two weeks ago when the citrus trees were flowering. Now I'm noticing that a lot of the small developing fruit has turned brown and dropped off. The remaining fruit has dark spots and seems stunted.
My tomato field has pigweed growing throughout, and it's already taller than my tomato plants in some areas. The unwanted plants are blocking sunlight and I'm concerned about harvest interference.
The roots of my carrot crop are being tunneled through by what looks like some kind of larvae. When I pull them up, there are winding galleries through the flesh and the carrots are unmarketable.

My bean plants have water-soaked lesions on the leaves and pods that turned brown and crusty. There's also a bacterial ooze visible on some pods in the morning, and the seeds inside affected pods are discolored and shriveled.

The corn stalks in the north field are turning pale green and the lower leaves have a purple tint along the edges. We've had adequate rainfall but the plants just aren't developing properly and the ears are small.

test.query: My pepper plants have brown, crispy leaf edges and the fruits are smaller than normal. The ground has been bone dry and cracked for the past month due to the heat wave and our well water is running low.

The strong winds we've been having all week have caused major problems in my pepper field. Plants are leaning heavily to one side, some have been uprooted entirely, and I'm seeing lots of blossom drop where flowers were torn off before they could set fruit.
My wheat field has been overtaken by wild grasses that are growing faster than my crop. They're competing for space and I'm worried my yield will suffer significantly if I don't address this soon.

Large sections of my cabbage heads are being eaten, leaving just the outer leaves with big irregular chunks missing. I spotted several green worms that match the color of the leaves perfectly hiding inside the heads.

My tomato plants have developed dark brown spots with yellow halos on the lower leaves, and the affected foliage is starting to curl and drop off. The spots seem to be spreading upward through the canopy over the past week.

My wheat plants are showing yellowing on the older leaves that's spreading upward, and the growth seems stunted compared to last season. The soil hasn't been amended in three years and I haven't applied any fertilizer this cycle.

The wheat crop is showing signs of early senescence with premature yellowing. Soil moisture readings have been consistently below 20% for three weeks now and the plants are shorter than they should be at this growth stage.

After yesterday's storm, my corn field looks like it's been beaten down. Stalks are broken and bent at weird angles, many are completely flattened. Some of the ears were knocked right off the plants and are lying in the mud.

Wild amaranth has emerged densely throughout my cotton field after the recent rains. The competition is severe and my cotton plants are not developing properly because these other plants are taking all the resources.
There are tiny white flies swarming up whenever I touch my bean plants. The leaves are turning yellow and have a sticky residue on them. I'm worried this is getting out of control.

My soybean plants have yellowing starting from the leaf tips and margins on older foliage, and overall the canopy looks lighter than it should. Growth rate has slowed noticeably in the past few weeks despite good weather conditions.
I'm seeing leaf drop on my cotton crop and the plants seem stunted. The field stays soggy because we've had constant rain for three weeks and the drainage isn't working properly in that low area.

The last three days have been brutally hot, over 105 degrees each afternoon. My lettuce crop has bolted prematurely and the leaves have this scorched, papery texture around the edges. Everything is wilting badly even though I've been irrigating.
Unwanted vegetation is choking out my soybean seedlings. These invasive plants are dense and growing aggressively, stealing moisture from the soil that my soybeans desperately need.

I'm seeing small holes appearing all over my tomato leaves and when I checked this morning there were green caterpillars crawling on the undersides. The damage is spreading quickly across several rows and some fruits are being eaten too.
I'm seeing interveinal chlorosis on my tomato plants - the veins stay green but the tissue between them is yellow. The newer growth looks affected and some leaves are curling at the tips.

I've noticed the cucumber vines are wilted and the leaves feel papery. When I dig down a few inches, the soil is completely saturated and smells swampy. This started after I increased my irrigation frequency last week.

My tomato plants were doing great until last night when temperatures dropped suddenly. This morning I found the leaves all blackened and wilted, especially on the younger plants near the top. The stems look water-soaked and translucent in places.
